title: SEGMENT 6 — The 1995 H. influenzae proof: shotgun works on a real genome

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — The 1995 H. influenzae proof: shotgun works on a real genome
I want to spend a short segment on a single 1995 paper, because that paper is a hinge point in the history of sequencing.
The paper is Fleischmann et al., 1995, in Science, titled "Whole-genome random sequencing and assembly of Haemophilus influenzae Rd.
" The authors are essentially the TIGR group — Robert Fleischmann, Mark Adams, Craig Venter, Hamilton Smith, and about thirty others.
What they did: they took the genome of Haemophilus influenzae, a small bacterial pathogen that causes ear infections and meningitis, total genome size 1.83 million base pairs, and they shotgunned it.

They sheared the DNA randomly, cloned the fragments into plasmid libraries, sequenced about twenty-eight thousand reads — at the time, an enormous number — and assembled the reads into a single circular chromosome using a TIGR-developed assembler.
The result: the first complete genome of a free-living organism, ever.
Period.
Full stop.
Why this matters for the Human Genome Project story is twofold.
First, it was a proof of concept.
Before 1995, shotgun assembly had been done on much smaller targets — individual cosmids, individual BACs — but nobody had ever shotgunned a whole genome and assembled it cleanly.

The skepticism within the public consortium was strong; many senior people, including Sulston and Lander, believed shotgun would fail catastrophically at the scale of a real chromosome because of the repeat problem.
The H. influenzae paper did not directly refute that — bacterial genomes have far fewer repeats than human — but it demonstrated that the wet-lab pipeline worked at the scale of a real organism.
Second, it gave Venter the credibility to propose Celera three years later.
Without the H. influenzae paper, Venter is a controversial figure with a track record of ESTs and patent fights.
With the H. influenzae paper, Venter is the man who actually delivered the first complete bacterial genome.
When he stood up in 1998 and said "I am going to shotgun the human genome," people had to take him seriously, because he had done it before, just at a thousand-fold smaller scale.
A small technical note that I want you to carry forward, because it will come up in the T2T segment.
The H. influenzae assembly was clean because bacterial genomes are mostly unique sequence — about ninety percent unique, ten percent repetitive at most.
The human genome is the opposite — roughly fifty percent repetitive, with massive blocks of near-identical repeats in centromeres and telomeres.
Shotgun assembly's performance degrades sharply with repeat content, which is why the Celera draft and the public draft both left the centromeres and telomeres unassembled.

Those regions remained gaps in the human reference until the Telomere-to-Telomere project finally closed them in 2022, using sequencing technologies — PacBio HiFi and Oxford Nanopore — that did not exist in 1995 or 2003.
That is the through-line.
Shotgun works.
Shotgun has limits.
Long reads fix the limits.
Long reads did not exist yet.
So the 1990s and 2000s genome was a clone-by-clone-plus-shotgun hybrid, and it had known gaps that would have to wait twenty years to be filled.
We have covered the proposal, the players, the rivalry, the two strategies, and the bacterial proof of concept.
Now we turn to the cultural revolution that the Human Genome Project produced almost as a byproduct: the Bermuda Principles, and the founding of modern biology's open-data culture.
